Paul stands before Festus, the new Roman procurator, and faces immediate pressure to be tried in Jerusalem—the very place where his enemies have laid a trap to murder him. Festus knows nothing of Paul's case and appears concerned only with maintaining political favor. He offers what seems like a reasonable compromise: move the trial to Jerusalem. But Paul sees clearly what Festus cannot. He recognizes that Roman law provides him protection precisely because he has done nothing wrong against Caesar's authority. So Paul invokes his right as a Roman citizen and appeals to Caesar himself. This is not an act of fear or desperation; it is a calculated, deliberate choice to trust the legal machinery of Rome rather than risk the murderous intentions of the Jewish leaders. What strikes us here is Paul's composure. He does not panic. He does not plead for mercy from this official who knows nothing about him. Instead, he appeals to a higher law and a distant emperor, confident that God's purposes for his life will be accomplished through the Roman system—the very system that minutes earlier had threatened to betray him.

Yet Festus remains puzzled. He has examined Paul carefully, and unlike his predecessor Felix, he is honest enough to admit that Paul has committed no crime against Rome. But the charges brought against him are baffling: they concern "some contentions with him regarding their own religion and a certain Jesus who had died, but whom Paul affirmed to be alive." Festus is utterly lost. He has no framework for evaluating the most important question before him—whether Jesus rose from the dead. To him, this is merely a dispute about Jewish religious matters, unworthy of his judicial attention. And so Festus, though just in his way, becomes an instrument of God's plan precisely by being inadequate to the case. He knows Paul is innocent. He knows the charges are baseless. He has nothing to report to Caesar. Yet he must send Paul to Rome anyway, because Paul has appealed and because Roman law demands it.

This chapter shows us that God often works His purposes through systems and individuals who do not know Him or understand His work. Festus is not corrupt; he is thoughtful and fair-minded. But he cannot grasp the truth that divides heaven and earth—the resurrection of Jesus Christ. The Jewish leaders resort to lies and violence, yet they accomplish nothing except moving Paul closer to the destiny God has appointed for him. Paul, meanwhile, moves forward with clear-eyed confidence, neither trusting in human favor nor fearing human threats. He knows that the only judgment that ultimately matters is God's judgment, and he lives accordingly. For us, this raises a question: Do we, like Paul, maintain our conviction about Jesus regardless of how powerful people respond to our testimony? Or do we, like Festus, treat the resurrection as interesting but essentially irrelevant to the serious business of life? The answer determines everything.
